domain_description: This PostgreSQL database indexes U.S. postal, geographic, and congressional data. Central entities include zip codes (zip_data) with detailed demographic, economic, and delivery statistics; states; congressional representatives; and Core Based Statistical Areas (CBSA). Supporting tables store zip-code aliases, area codes, bad aliases, counties per zip, and zip-to-congressional-district mappings. The schema links zip_data to states, CBSAs, aliases, area codes, and bad aliases; countries link states and zip codes; and zip_congress links zip codes to congressional districts. The database can answer questions about area codes by county or state, postal points under a congress representative, bad aliases, and aliases for a given zip code.

term_glossary.CBSA: Core Based Statistical Area, a U.S. geographic region for metro/micro areas.
term_glossary.CID: Congressional ID, likely a unique identifier for a representative.
term_glossary.multi_county: Indicates if a zip code covers multiple counties.
term_glossary.state_fips: Federal Information Processing Standard code for a state.
term_glossary.1st_quarter_payroll: Total payroll for the first quarter of the year.
term_glossary.total_beneficiaries: Number of Social Security beneficiaries in the area.
term_glossary.monthly_benefits_all: Total monthly Social Security benefits paid.

domain_description: This database stores airline flight data, including details about flights and references to air carriers and airports. The main entities are Air Carriers (identified by code), Airports (identified by code), and Airlines (a table recording individual flight legs with times, delays, cancellations, and origins/destinations). The declared relationships link the Airlines table to Airports via its ORIGIN and DEST fields. The database can answer questions about flight operators, delays, cancellations, origins, destinations, and specific dates, such as identifying carrier descriptions for flights to a city, counting flights by operator on a certain date, finding the carrier with most cancellations, computing percentage of early departures for a given carrier, or locating the origin of the fastest flight.

term_glossary.CRS_DEP_TIME: Scheduled departure time in HHMM format.
term_glossary.NAS_DELAY: Delay attributed to national air system.
term_glossary.LATE_AIRCRAFT_DELAY: Delay due to late arrival of previous leg.

domain_description: This database stores information about mobile applications from the Google Play Store, including app details such as name, category, rating, number of reviews, size, installs, type (free/paid), price, content rating, and genres. It also contains user reviews for these apps, with fields for the review text, sentiment (positive, negative, or neutral), sentiment polarity score, and sentiment subjectivity score. A foreign key relationship links user reviews to their corresponding app by app name. The database can answer questions about app install counts, sentiment scores, review sentiment breakdowns by app or content rating, and relationships like finding the app with highest sentiment polarity or the number of negative comments on free apps.

term_glossary.Rating: User rating of the app, typically on a scale of 1 to 5.
term_glossary.Reviews: Count of user reviews for the app.
term_glossary.Installs: Number of installs or downloads of the app.
term_glossary.Type: Whether the app is free or paid.
term_glossary.Genres: Category or genre classification of the app.
term_glossary.Sentiment: Label for review sentiment: Positive, Negative, or Neutral.
term_glossary.Sentiment_Subjectivity: Numerical score indicating how subjective the review text is.

domain_description: This database catalogues academic publications, including papers, authors, conferences, and journals. It can answer queries about paper titles, publication years, author affiliations, conference short names, journal homepages, and relationships between papers and their authors. The schema links papers to conferences and journals via foreign keys, and associates authors with papers through a join table. Sample questions involve filtering papers by year, computing average publication counts per journal, finding authors by paper title, and counting authors by affiliation.

This database tracks root beer brands, products, customer purchases, and reviews. It includes entities for customers, root beer brands, specific root beer containers, purchase locations, geolocation coordinates, and transactions. Relationships link customers to their reviews and transactions, brands to their root beers, and locations to geolocation data. The schema can answer questions about customer purchase behavior, brand characteristics (e.g., sweeteners, caffeine, alcohol, container types), sales by location, and review ratings. Sample questions involve specific brands like Henry Weinhard's, customer names like Nicholas Sparks, payment methods like Visa, and location names like Sac State American River Courtyard.

term_glossary.geolocation: Table storing latitude and longitude coordinates for locations.
term_glossary.rootbeer: Table for individual root beer container records, referencing brand and location.
term_glossary.rootbeerreview: Table linking customers and brands with star ratings and review text.

domain_description: This database supports a bike-sharing retrieval index, tracking station inventory, trip records, and weather data. It can answer questions about station details (location, dock count), trip durations and subscriptions, real-time bike and dock availability, and weather conditions. Sample questions involve mean humidity for a specific trip, subscriber trip details from a given station on a specific date, peak bike availability times, bike borrow counts at specific timestamps, and trip duration thresholds. No relationships are declared between entities.

term_glossary.dock_count: Number of docking points at the station
term_glossary.subscription_type: Type of user subscription for the trip

domain_description: This database supports a publishing company's operations. It tracks authors (with contact details), publishers, book titles (including price, advance, royalty, sales), and stores where books are sold. Employee information is recorded, with job descriptions and levels. Sales transactions link stores, titles, and quantities, with discounts applied per store. Royalty schedules define royalty percentages for quantity ranges. The database can answer queries about publisher locations for specific books, average employee job levels, comparative store sales, peak order dates, and titles with highest royalty percentages and their applicable quantity ranges. Key entities: authors, publishers, titles, stores, employees, jobs, sales, discounts, royalty schedules, and title-author assignments.

term_glossary.roysched: Table storing royalty percentages for title quantity ranges.
term_glossary.payterms: Payment terms for a sale (e.g., Net 30).
term_glossary.advance: Advance payment paid to author for a title.
term_glossary.ytd_sales: Year-to-date sales quantity for a title.
term_glossary.contract: Indicates whether author is under contract (text likely boolean).
term_glossary.au_ord: Order of author for a title in multi-author works.
term_glossary.royaltyper: Royalty percentage allocated to a specific author for a title.
term_glossary.lorange: Lower bound of quantity range for royalty schedule.
term_glossary.hirange: Upper bound of quantity range for royalty schedule.
term_glossary.discounttype: Type or category of discount (e.g., wholesale, promotional).

domain_description: This database supports a book retail or publishing operation. It tracks books, their authors, publishers, languages, and page counts, along with customer addresses and order history. The schema includes entities for customers, addresses, orders, order lines, shipping methods, and order statuses. Foreign key relationships link books to publishers and languages, book-author associations, and customer addresses to customers and address records. The database can answer questions about book details (e.g., publisher by title), customer locations (e.g., streets in a city), book counts by publisher and page range, and order status updates (e.g., returns in a given year).

term_glossary.isbn13: 13-digit International Standard Book Number for a book.
term_glossary.num_pages: Number of pages in the book.
term_glossary.dest_address_id: Destination address identifier for an order.

domain_description: This database stores information about California public schools, including their locations, contact details, and operational status. It also contains student performance data from SAT exams and records of free/reduced-price meal program participation, which serves as a proxy for socioeconomic status. The main entities are schools, satscores, and frpm. The database can answer questions about school characteristics (e.g., charter status, virtual instruction, county/district), test participation and scores, and meal program counts. Relationships link both satscores and frpm to schools via their CDSCode.

term_glossary.CDSCode: California Department of Education school identifier code.
term_glossary.frpm: Free/Reduced Price Meal program data table.
term_glossary.IRC: Income Reporting Code for meal eligibility.
term_glossary.NumTstTakr: Number of students who took the SAT test.
term_glossary.NumGE1500: Number of students scoring 1500 or above on SAT.
term_glossary.NCESDist: National Center for Education Statistics district ID.
term_glossary.NCESSchool: National Center for Education Statistics school ID.
term_glossary.GSoffered: Grade span offered by the school.

domain_description: This database supports a classic car sales company, tracking offices, employees, customers, orders, payments, product lines, and products. It can answer questions about customer orders and payments, employee assignments to offices and customers, product inventory, and order statuses. Sample questions involve finding office addresses for sales representatives, listing customers with disputed orders, identifying UK sales reps with low credit limits, and querying payment details for specific customers or time periods.

term_glossary.MSRP: Manufacturer's suggested retail price for a product.
term_glossary.reportsTo: Employee number of the manager this employee reports to.
term_glossary.salesRepEmployeeNumber: Employee number of the sales representative assigned to a customer.
term_glossary.buyPrice: Cost price the company pays to acquire the product.
term_glossary.productLine: Category or product line to which a product belongs.
term_glossary.orderLineNumber: Line number of an item within an order.

domain_description: This database indexes Magic: The Gathering cards, providing details on card attributes, artist information, set membership, legalities in game formats, and rulings. The main entities are cards, sets, foreign data (translations), legalities, set_translations, and rulings. Users can query card counts by frame effect, types illustrated by an artist, cards with a specific converted mana cost in a set, rulings for a particular card, or find the top-cost card without multiple faces. Relationships link rulings, legalities, and foreign data to cards via uuid, and set_translations to sets via setCode.

term_glossary.convertedManaCost: Total mana value of the card
term_glossary.faceConvertedManaCost: Mana value of a specific face on multi-face cards
term_glossary.isAlternative: Whether the card is an alternate version
term_glossary.isFullArt: Whether the card has full-art illustration
term_glossary.isOnlineOnly: Whether the card exists only in digital formats
term_glossary.isPromo: Whether the card is a promotional printing
term_glossary.isReprint: Whether the card is a reprint
term_glossary.isReserved: Whether the card is on the reserved list
term_glossary.isStarter: Whether the card is a starter deck exclusive
term_glossary.isStorySpotlight: Whether the card features a story spotlight
term_glossary.isTextless: Whether the card has textless art
term_glossary.uuid: Universally unique identifier for the card

domain_description: This database stores information about cars, including their prices, technical specifications (such as miles per gallon, horsepower, weight, and acceleration), production details (model year and country of origin), and country names. It can answer questions about the most expensive cars, cars with high horsepower, fuel efficiency, car names by price, and the distribution of cars by country. The schema links car data to prices and production records, and production records to countries.

term_glossary.origin: Foreign key referencing the country of production.
term_glossary.mpg: Miles per gallon, a measure of fuel efficiency.
term_glossary.displacement: Engine displacement, typically in liters or cubic inches.
term_glossary.car_name: Name or model designation of the car.

domain_description: This database documents crime incidents in Chicago, linking each crime report to geographic and administrative divisions. Central entities include Crime records with details like date, arrest status, and location; Community_Area and Neighborhood for population-level geography; Districts and Wards for police and political boundaries; and code tables (IUCR, FBI_Code) providing standardized crime categories. The schema supports queries about crime counts by area, population, neighborhood, district commander, and crime descriptions based on location type (e.g., aircraft).

term_glossary.IUCR: Illinois Uniform Crime Reporting code for classifying crimes.
term_glossary.fbi_code_no: FBI code identifying a specific crime category.
term_glossary.iucr_no: Primary key referencing the IUCR code table.
term_glossary.beat: Police beat number, a small patrol area.

domain_description: This database stores information about academic papers and their citation relationships. The 'paper' table records each paper's unique identifier and a class label (e.g., 'AI'). The 'cites' table tracks which papers cite which other papers using cited and citing paper IDs. The 'content' table links each paper to the words it cites, via a word identifier. A declared foreign key connects content.paper_id to paper.paper_id. The database can answer questions about word citation counts, papers that cite a specific word, and word usage within a given class label, as illustrated by sample queries.

term_glossary.class_label: Category or topic label assigned to a paper (e.g., 'AI').
term_glossary.word_cited_id: Identifier for a word that is cited in a paper.

domain_description: This database indexes software repositories and their solutions, methods, and parameters for retrieval. The main entities are Repo (repository metadata), Solution (a file or component within a repo), Method (a function or procedure within a solution), and MethodParameter (arguments to a method). The schema supports questions about repository statistics (stars, forks, watchers), solution processing times, method details (names, tokenized names, comments), and relationships such as linking methods to solutions and solutions to repositories. Sample questions ask for repository IDs with specific method names, counts of methods sharing tokenized names, solution counts per repository, comparisons of stars between solution repositories, and URLs for repos with the longest processed-time solution.

term_glossary.FullComment: Complete comment string attached to a method.
term_glossary.CommentIsXml: Flag indicating whether the comment is in XML format.
term_glossary.SampledAt: Timestamp or version marker when the method was sampled.
term_glossary.ProcessedTime: Unix timestamp or integer representing when the entity was processed.

domain_description: This database is a retrieval index for a Q&A platform similar to Stack Exchange. It stores users, their posts (questions and answers), comments, votes, tags, badges, and post history. Key entities include users with reputation and display names, posts with scores and tags, comments with scores, and badges earned by users. The schema tracks relationships such as users authoring posts and comments, posts linked as duplicates or references, and badges assigned to users. Questions can be answered about user demographics (e.g., finding adults), post statistics (e.g., most answered post by a specific user), total scores for edits by a user, badge names earned by particular users, and counts of positive comments.

term_glossary.PostHistoryTypeId: Code indicating the type of change recorded in post history
term_glossary.LinkTypeId: Code representing the relationship type between linked posts
term_glossary.PostTypeId: Code distinguishing questions, answers, or other post types
term_glossary.AcceptedAnswerId: Post ID of the answer accepted by the question author
term_glossary.ExcerptPostId: Post ID containing the tag excerpt
term_glossary.WikiPostId: Post ID containing the tag wiki
term_glossary.ViewCount: Number of times the post has been viewed

domain_description: This PostgreSQL database stores information about cryptocurrency coins and their historical market data. The 'coins' table contains static details such as coin name, slug, symbol, status, category, description, tags, website, platform, and add/launch dates. The 'historical' table records daily trading metrics including price, market cap, volume, rank, supply figures, and percentage changes. Queries can answer questions about specific coin values on past dates, coins meeting certain criteria like tags or addition dates, and comparative performance metrics such as price changes. No explicit relationships are declared between the tables.

term_glossary.cmc_rank: CoinMarketCap rank position of the coin
term_glossary.num_market_pairs: Number of market pairs the coin is traded on
term_glossary.percent_change_1h: Price change percentage over the last hour
term_glossary.percent_change_24h: Price change percentage over the last 24 hours
term_glossary.percent_change_7d: Price change percentage over the last 7 days
term_glossary.time_high: Timestamp when coin reached its highest price of the day
term_glossary.time_low: Timestamp when coin reached its lowest price of the day
term_glossary.platform_id: Identifier for the blockchain platform the coin is built on

domain_description: This database stores information about higher education institutions and their graduation outcomes. The main entity, institution_details, contains attributes of individual colleges and universities (e.g., location, control, student count, graduation rates, financial aid). Two entities capture disaggregated graduation data: institution_grads (per institution, year, gender, race) and state_sector_grads (aggregated by state, control, level). state_sector_details provides summary metrics for each state-sector combination. Questions can be answered about graduate counts by gender, race, and time period; institutional characteristics; and state-level comparisons. Relationships link grad tables to details tables via unitid and stateid, and also join states across tables.

term_glossary.hbcu: Historically Black College or University flag
term_glossary.flagship: Indicator if institution is a flagship university
term_glossary.pell_value: Percentage of students receiving Pell Grants

domain_description: This database supports a university course and advising retrieval index. It tracks courses with their academic level (e.g., basic, medium, professional, master/graduate) and persons who can be professors or students, with attributes like position, phase, and years in program. The schema captures two key relationships: advising connections between persons (who advises whom) via the advisedBy table, and teaching assignments linking courses to persons via the taughtBy table. It can answer questions such as the average number of courses taught per professor per level, the count of courses at specific levels, the advisor of a given student and the level of courses they teach, the program year of students with multiple advisors, and the IDs of courses belonging to particular level categories.

term_glossary.p_id_dummy: Second foreign key to person, used for the advisor in advisedBy.
term_glossary.courseLevel: Classification of course difficulty or audience, e.g., basic or professional.
term_glossary.hasPosition: Title or role held by a person within the program.
term_glossary.inPhase: Academic phase or stage a person is currently in.
term_glossary.yearsInProgram: Number of years a person has been in the academic program.

domain_description: This PostgreSQL database stores a collection of recipes and their nutritional information. The main entities are Recipe (titles, preparation times, directions), Ingredient (names and categories), Nutrition (calories, fat, vitamins, etc.), and Quantity (linking ingredients to recipes with amounts and units). Declared foreign keys connect Nutrition and Quantity to Recipe, and Quantity to Ingredient. The database can answer questions about recipe calorie counts, vitamin content, specific ingredient usage, and recipes linked to health effects like constipation, as seen in sample user queries.

term_glossary.stnd_min: Standing or resting time in minutes.

domain_description: This database catalogs breweries and the beers they produce, tracking details such as location and beer attributes. The main entities are breweries (with name, city, state) and beers (linked to a brewery by brewery_id). It can answer questions about beer characteristics like alcohol content (abv), bitterness (ibu), style, and volume per serving, enabling comparisons across breweries and locations. The sample questions involve average abv per bottle, regional bitterness extremes, and states with least bitter beer.

term_glossary.abv: Alcohol by volume, a measure of alcohol content.
term_glossary.ibu: International Bitterness Units, a measure of beer bitterness.
term_glossary.ounces: Volume of the beer in fluid ounces.

domain_description: This database models an academic system with entities for courses, professors, students, registrations (enrollment and grades), and research assistantships (RA). Courses have attributes like name, credits, and difficulty. Professors have gender, name, email, popularity, teaching ability, and alma mater. Students have name, phone, email, intelligence, GPA, and type. Registrations link students to courses with a grade and SAT score. RA links students to professors as advisors with a salary field. The schema supports queries about student performance, professor effectiveness, course difficulty, and advising relationships, such as average course satisfaction, failure rates by GPA, professor teaching ability impacts on advisee salaries, and student course histories.

term_glossary.RA: Research assistant table linking students to advisors

domain_description: This database records fuel station transactions for a retail chain. It includes customers (with segment and currency), gas stations (with chain and country), and products (like fuel types). The central table 'transactions_1k' links these entities with purchase details (amount, price, date/time). A 'yearmonth' table tracks monthly consumption per customer. The data can answer questions about station counts by country/product, customer spending in given currencies, top spenders on specific dates, and consumption thresholds. Relationships: yearmonth.CustomerID references customers.CustomerID.

term_glossary.Segment: Market or customer segment classification (e.g., premium, basic).
term_glossary.ChainID: Identifier for a gas station chain or brand.
term_glossary.CardID: Identifier for the payment card used in a transaction.
term_glossary.Consumption: Total consumption amount for a customer in a given month.

domain_description: This database stores information about Disney movies, including characters, directors, and box office gross. The main entities are characters (with movie title, release date, hero, villain, and song), director (linking a name to a movie title), and movies_total_gross (with genre, MPAA rating, and gross figures). A separate revenue table tracks yearly studio earnings. Declared relationships link director names to movie titles and movie gross records to character entries. The database can answer questions about movie heroes, villains, directors, and box office performance, such as identifying the director of a specific film or the villain of a popular movie.

term_glossary.MPAA_rating: Motion Picture Association of America film rating (e.g., G, PG, PG-13).
term_glossary.inflation_adjusted_gross: Box office gross adjusted for inflation to compare historical earnings.

domain_description: This database supports a retrieval index for educational project donations. It captures details about school projects seeking funding, the essays that describe them, donations made by donors, and the resources requested. The main entities are projects, essays, donations, and resources. Projects are linked to schools and teachers, and include information such as grade level, poverty level, and focus area. Donations track donor details, amounts, and payment methods. Resources list items needed for each project. The schema can answer questions about project characteristics, donor behavior, geographic distributions, and resource types. Declared relationships connect donations and resources to projects via foreign keys.

term_glossary.school_ncesid: National Center for Education Statistics identifier for schools
term_glossary.school_metro: Metropolitan area classification of the school
term_glossary.school_nlns: Indicates if school is part of New Leaders for New Schools network
term_glossary.school_kipp: Indicates if school is a KIPP charter school
term_glossary.school_charter_ready_promise: Indicates if school has Charter Ready Promise status
term_glossary.fulfillment_labor_materials: Costs for labor and materials to fulfill the project
term_glossary.is_teacher_acct: Flag indicating if donor is a teacher account
term_glossary.via_giving_page: Whether donation was made through a giving page
term_glossary.for_honoree: Whether donation was made in honor of someone
term_glossary.eligible_double_your_impact_match: Whether project qualifies for double impact matching
term_glossary.eligible_almost_home_match: Whether project qualifies for Almost Home matching

domain_description: This database stores football match results across various divisions. The main entities are divisions (with a textual primary key, name, and country) and matches (with division code, date, home/away team names, full-time home and away goals, full-time result, and season). A declared foreign key links matchs.Div to divisions.division. The database can answer questions about match outcomes, goal counts, and specific divisions or dates, such as home team victories, away victories, draws, or total goals by a team across seasons.

term_glossary.FTHG: Full-time home goals, integer count of goals by the home team.
term_glossary.FTAG: Full-time away goals, integer count of goals by the away team.
term_glossary.FTR: Full-time result, typically H/A/D for home/away/draw.
term_glossary.Div: Division code referencing the divisions table; an opaque abbreviation.

domain_description: This database records football (soccer) leagues, teams, players, and matches. Key entities include Player, Player_Attributes, Team, Team_Attributes, League, Country, and Match. It can answer questions about player skills (e.g., finishing), team playing styles (e.g., build-up play passing class), physical attributes (e.g., height), and match details (e.g., goals, betting odds). Relationships link players to their attributes, teams to their attributes, leagues to countries, and matches to participating teams and players. The sample questions involve querying team attributes by date, player attributes by skill and age, and listing teams by a specific attribute class.

term_glossary.B365H: Bet365 odds for home team win
term_glossary.B365A: Bet365 odds for away team win
term_glossary.BWH: Bet&Win odds for home team win
term_glossary.BWA: Bet&Win odds for away team win
term_glossary.IWH: Interwetten odds for home team win
term_glossary.IWA: Interwetten odds for away team win
term_glossary.LBH: Ladbrokes odds for home team win
term_glossary.LBA: Ladbrokes odds for away team win
term_glossary.PSH: Pinnacle Sports odds for home team win
term_glossary.PSA: Pinnacle Sports odds for away team win
term_glossary.WHH: William Hill odds for home team win
term_glossary.WHA: William Hill odds for away team win
term_glossary.SJH: SJ odds for home team win
term_glossary.SJA: SJ odds for away team win
term_glossary.VCH: VC Bet odds for home team win
term_glossary.VCA: VC Bet odds for away team win
term_glossary.GBH: Gamebookers odds for home team win
term_glossary.GBA: Gamebookers odds for away team win
term_glossary.BSH: Blue Square odds for home team win
term_glossary.BSA: Blue Square odds for away team win

domain_description: This PostgreSQL database models a bank's operations, including clients, accounts, cards, transactions, loans, orders, and districts. Clients are linked to accounts via disposition records (disp), which specify whether the client is an owner or disponent. Accounts are associated with a district, and transactions, loans, and orders are recorded per account. Cards are linked to a specific disposition. The database can answer questions about client demographics (e.g., birth year, gender, district region), account activity (e.g., transactions from a specific district), loan details, card types (e.g., junior credit cards), and account opening dates. Relationships include account-district, client-district, disp-client, disp-account, card-disp, loan-account, order-account, and trans-account.

term_glossary.disp: links client to account with type (owner/disponent)

domain_description: This database tracks business inspections and violations, primarily for eateries. It contains information about businesses (name, address, city, etc.), their inspection results (score, date, type), and any violations found (type, risk category, description). The database can answer queries about business locations, inspection dates and scores, and violation risk levels, such as counting low-risk violations in a given year or identifying businesses in a specific city.

term_glossary.business_certificate: License or certificate number for the business
term_glossary.application_date: Date when the business applied for a license
term_glossary.tax_code: Tax classification code for the business
term_glossary.risk_category: Risk level of a violation (e.g., low, medium, high)
term_glossary.violation_type_id: Code identifying the type of violation

domain_description: This database tracks food establishment inspections. It stores information about establishments (with licenses), employees who perform inspections, inspections (type, date, results), inspection points (specific criteria checked with codes and fines), and violations linking inspections to points. Relationships connect inspections to the employee and establishment involved, and violations to inspection points and inspections. The schema can answer questions about inspection results by employee, inspection counts by category, inspection types on specific dates, and business compliance over time, such as which wards have multiple failed inspections.

term_glossary.dba_name: Doing business as name, the operating name of the establishment.
term_glossary.aka_name: Also known as, an alternative name for the establishment.
term_glossary.ward: A political district or administrative division within a city.
term_glossary.inspection_type: Category or purpose of the inspection (e.g., routine, follow-up).
term_glossary.followup_to: Points to the ID of a prior inspection that this inspection follows.

domain_description: This database records historical Formula One racing data. It centers on drivers, constructors (teams), circuits, seasons, and races. Key facts include race results, qualifying performance, lap times, pit stops, and championship standings for both drivers and constructors. Relationships link races to their circuit and season; results, standings, lap times, pit stops, and qualifying to the relevant race, driver, and constructor. The database can answer queries about driver biographical details (e.g., nationality, date of birth), fastest lap times and speeds, pit stop laps for specific drivers, and performance comparisons across specific Grands Prix and seasons.

term_glossary.circuitRef: Short reference code for a circuit.
term_glossary.constructorRef: Short reference code for a constructor (team).
term_glossary.driverRef: Short reference code for a driver.
term_glossary.dob: Date of birth of the driver.
term_glossary.forename: Given first name of the driver.
term_glossary.positionText: Textual representation of finishing position (e.g., R for retired).

domain_description: This database captures genetic information, focusing on genes, their classifications, and pairwise interactions. The main entities are Classification (assigning a localization to each gene), Genes (detailing properties such as essentiality, class, complex, phenotype, motif, chromosome, function, and localization), and Interactions (recording pairs of genes with an interaction type and expression correlation score). Declared relationships link both Genes and Interactions to Classification via GeneID, ensuring each gene has a defined localization. Users can query for genes based on chromosome number, cellular localization, function, or class (e.g., motorproteins), and analyze interaction pairs by expression correlation (positive or negative) or interaction type. The schema supports questions about gene characteristics and the strength and nature of their interactions.

term_glossary.Essential: Indicates whether the gene is essential for organism survival.
term_glossary.Class: Classification of the gene, e.g., motorprotein or other functional group.
term_glossary.Complex: Protein complex that the gene product is part of.
term_glossary.Motif: Sequence motif or domain present in the gene.
term_glossary.Expression_Corr: Correlation coefficient of expression levels between interacting genes.
term_glossary.Type: Type of interaction between the two genes (e.g., physical, genetic).

domain_description: This database tracks professional hockey teams, players, coaches, and awards. Its central entities are Teams, Master (players/coaches), Coaches, and various statistics tables (Scoring, Goalies, etc.). It also includes Hall of Fame (HOF), awards (AwardsPlayers, AwardsCoaches, AwardsMisc), and postseason series (SeriesPost). Declared relationships link players to teams and years for performance records, coaches to their teams, and award recipients to Masters or Coaches. The database can answer queries about player career paths (e.g., goalies who later became coaches and Hall of Famers), team performance, player physical attributes (total weights), coach origins, and goalie statistics like postseason shots against.

term_glossary.stint: Number indicating a player's tenure segment in a season.

domain_description: This database supports a human resources retrieval index for an organization. It contains information about employees, their job positions, and office locations. The main entities are employee, position, and location. Employees are linked to a position via a foreign key and to a location via another foreign key. The database can answer questions about employee counts and performance in specific cities, educational requirements for positions, location cities in certain regions, employee details filtered by state, and salary ranges for named employees.

term_glossary.ssn: Social Security Number, used as unique employee identifier

domain_description: This PostgreSQL database supports a hockey player and statistics retrieval index. It tracks player biographical details (name, birth date, birthplace, nationality, draft info, career summary metrics) in PlayerInfo, along with separate lookup tables for height (in cm and inch strings) and weight (in kg and lbs). Season-specific performance stats (games played, goals, assists, points, penalty minutes, plus/minus) for each player, team, league, and game type are stored in SeasonStatus. The declared foreign keys link PlayerInfo.height to height_info.height_id, PlayerInfo.weight to weight_info.weight_id, and SeasonStatus.ELITEID to PlayerInfo.ELITEID. The database can answer questions about player heights (e.g., exact height in inch format, tallest on a team), performance by team and season, draft outcomes, and penalty infractions among drafted players.

term_glossary.ELITEID: Unique identifier for each player in the PlayerInfo table.
term_glossary.CSS_rank: Central Scouting Service rank for draft-eligible players.
term_glossary.sum_7yr_GP: Total games played over a 7-year career window.
term_glossary.sum_7yr_TOI: Total time on ice over a 7-year career window.
term_glossary.GAMETYPE: Type of game, e.g., regular season or playoff.
term_glossary.PLUSMINUS: Plus/minus statistic measuring goal differential while on ice.

domain_description: This database stores information about images, their objects, attributes, and relationships. Each image (IMG_OBJ) contains multiple objects, each with a sample ID, object class (OBJ_CLASSES), and bounding box coordinates (X, Y, W, H). Objects can have attributes from a fixed set (ATT_CLASSES), recorded in IMG_OBJ_ATT. Relationships between objects (IMG_REL) use a predicate class (PRED_CLASSES) to describe interactions (e.g., spatial or semantic relations). Users can query for object attributes, find images with specific attribute-object combinations, retrieve coordinates of objects with particular attributes, or list relations involving a given object sample.

term_glossary.ATT_CLASS: a type of attribute (e.g., color, texture) that an object can have
term_glossary.OBJ_CLASS: a type of object (e.g., bear, picture) present in an image
term_glossary.PRED_CLASS: a type of relation predicate (e.g., 'on', 'next to') between two objects
term_glossary.OBJ_SAMPLE_ID: unique identifier for an object instance within a single image

domain_description: The database indexes multilingual Wikipedia-like content, tracking pages, words, and biwords (two-word sequences) per language. Core entities are languages (with locale, page/word counts), pages (with title, revision, word count), and words (with total occurrence count). Junction tables record occurrences of words in languages and in pages, and biwords track co-occurrence of ordered word pairs per language. Foreign keys link pages, words, and biwords to languages. The schema supports queries about word and biword frequencies, page lengths, language locales, and page counts for given languages or word pairs.

term_glossary.lid: language identifier, foreign key to langs
term_glossary.pid: page identifier, foreign key to pages
term_glossary.wid: word identifier, foreign key to words
term_glossary.w1st: first word in a biword pair, foreign key to words
term_glossary.w2nd: second word in a biword pair, foreign key to words
term_glossary.biwords: table recording occurrence counts of ordered two-word sequences

domain_description: This database indexes episodes of TV series, including metadata like titles, air dates, ratings, and summaries. It tracks people (actors, crew) with biographical details such as height and birthplace. Awards given to episodes or people are recorded, along with credit roles for each episode. Users can query episode counts per season, average cast size per season, actor heights in award-winning episodes, award counts for specific episodes, most frequent appearances, and award winners for specific organizations. Relationships link keywords, awards, credits, and votes to episodes, and awards and credits to persons.

term_glossary.episode_id: Unique identifier for a TV episode.
term_glossary.person_id: Unique identifier for a person (actor, crew).
term_glossary.votes: Number of user votes for the episode.
term_glossary.percent: Percentage of votes at a given star level.

domain_description: This PostgreSQL database stores biographical and identification information for United States legislators, with separate tables for current and historical members. Each table contains fields such as names, birth dates, gender, religion, and numerous identifiers linking to external databases (e.g., Ballotpedia, FEC, GovTrack, OpenSecrets, Wikipedia). The database can answer questions about current and past legislators, compare service durations, filter by demographics like gender or birth year, and retrieve official names or external account details. No relationships between tables are declared. Sample questions involve comparing term counts, finding gender-based differences, looking up Facebook usernames, and identifying longest-serving or oldest legislators.

term_glossary.ballotpedia_id: Identifier for the Ballotpedia database entry.
term_glossary.bioguide_id: Identifier from the Biographical Directory of the U.S. Congress.
term_glossary.fec_id: Identifier from the Federal Election Commission.
term_glossary.gender_bio: Gender field sourced from biographical data.
term_glossary.google_entity_id_id: Identifier for Google Knowledge Graph entity.
term_glossary.house_history_id: Identifier from the House History database.
term_glossary.icpsr_id: Identifier from the Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research.
term_glossary.lis_id: Identifier for the Legislative Information System.
term_glossary.thomas_id: Identifier from the THOMAS legislative database.
term_glossary.wikidata_id: Identifier for the Wikidata knowledge base.
term_glossary.bioguide_previous_id: Previous Biographical Directory identifier for a legislator.
term_glossary.house_history_alternate_id: Alternate identifier from the House History database.

domain_description: This database stores survey data, including questions, answers, and survey metadata. The main entities are Question (with text and ID), Survey (with ID and description), and Answer (with text, survey ID, user ID, and question ID). It can answer questions about respondent demographics, survey participation by year or location, and response frequencies for specific questions. No relationships are declared between entities.

term_glossary.SurveyID: Identifier for a specific survey instance.
term_glossary.UserID: Identifier for a respondent or participant.
term_glossary.QuestionID: Identifier for a specific question in the survey.

domain_description: This database catalogs historical menus and their dishes, supporting queries about dish appearances across menus, menu pricing, and menu composition. The main entities are Dish (dish details and appearance statistics), Menu (metadata like venue, event, and page/dish counts), MenuPage (individual pages within a menu with image dimensions), and MenuItem (links dishes to menu pages with price and position). Relationships connect MenuPage to Menu, MenuItem to MenuPage, and MenuItem to Dish. Sample questions address average menu page numbers for specific dishes, total item costs per menu, counts of menus that did not support take-out or booking, dish counts per menu by name, and average dishes per page for a given menu.

term_glossary.menus_appeared: Number of distinct menus that feature this dish.
term_glossary.times_appeared: Total number of occurrences of this dish across all menus.
term_glossary.first_appeared: Earliest year the dish appeared on a menu.
term_glossary.last_appeared: Most recent year the dish appeared on a menu.
term_glossary.lowest_price: Cheapest recorded price for this dish on any menu.
term_glossary.highest_price: Most expensive recorded price for this dish on any menu.
term_glossary.call_number: Library classification identifier assigned to the menu.
term_glossary.xpos: Horizontal pixel coordinate of the menu item on the page.
term_glossary.ypos: Vertical pixel coordinate of the menu item on the page.

domain_description: This database stores geographical, socioeconomic, and political data about the world. It includes entities such as countries, cities, provinces, rivers, lakes, seas, mountains, islands, deserts, and continents. Fields cover area, population, GDP, economic sectors, inflation, border lengths, river lengths, and demographic metrics. Queries can involve comparing border lengths, calculating continent GDP, listing rivers by length, or determining land area percentages. No explicit relationships are declared between entities.

This database stores information about movies, actors, and the characters they play in films. It is designed for a retrieval index that can answer questions about film genres, ratings, financial data (budget and gross), release dates, actor demographics (name, birth city, height, ethnicity, net worth), and character details (name, credit order, pay, screen time). Relationships link characters to both movies and actors via foreign keys. Sample queries involve finding the genre of a PG-rated movie starring the highest-net-worth actor, identifying which movie a specific character appears in, computing the percentage of US actors in a film's credits, retrieving a character by credit order, and finding an actor who played a given role in a specific drama film.

term_glossary.CreditOrder: Position of the character in the film's credit list

domain_description: This PostgreSQL database supports a DVD rental business. It stores information about films, actors, categories, languages, customers, staff, stores, and rental transactions. Key entities include film (with details like replacement cost, rental rate, length, and special features), actor, customer, staff, store, and rental/payment records. The database can answer questions about costs, film attributes, actor involvement, customer activity per store, and rental payments. The sample questions relate to film categories, replacement costs, store rental payment comparisons, customer rental history at a specific store, and film pricing per day with special features.

term_glossary.film_text: Stores a copy of film title and description for full-text search
term_glossary.last_update: Timestamp of the most recent change to a record
term_glossary.special_features: Descriptive text listing DVD extras (e.g., trailers, commentaries)
term_glossary.rental_rate: Cost to rent the film for the standard rental duration
term_glossary.replacement_cost: Fee charged if the film is lost or not returned
term_glossary.rental_duration: Number of days a rental is allowed before late fees apply

domain_description: This database supports a movie retrieval index, tracking movies, user-created lists, and ratings. The main entities are movies (with titles, release years, directors, and popularity), lists (with titles, descriptions, and follower counts), and ratings (with scores and critic details). Users are represented through ratings_users and lists_users, linking to lists and ratings. The schema can answer questions about movie ratings by user, directors with many films in a date range, list titles created by a specific user, and the most recently updated list.

term_glossary.user_trialist: Indicates if the user is on a trial account.
term_glossary.user_subscriber: Indicates if the user has an active subscription.
term_glossary.user_eligible_for_trial: Shows whether the user qualifies for a trial.
term_glossary.user_has_payment_method: Indicates if the user has a payment method on file.
term_glossary.critic: Indicates whether the rating was made by a critic.

domain_description: This database stores information about a movie platform, including users, directors, actors, and movies. It captures user attributes (age, gender, occupation), director characteristics (quality, average revenue), actor attributes (gender, quality), and movie details (year, language, country, running time). Junction tables link movies to their actors and directors, including cast order and genre. A rating table connects users to movies with ratings. The database can answer queries about movie cast members (e.g., in English movies with specific running times), director influence on ratings, user preferences by age, movie filtering by running time and revenue, and counts of recent dramas or action films.

term_glossary.avg_revenue: Average revenue generated by the director's films.
term_glossary.cast_num: Order or number of the actor in the movie cast.
term_glossary.isEnglish: Indicates if the movie is in English (yes/no).
term_glossary.runningtime: Duration of the movie in minutes.
term_glossary.u2base: Table linking users to movies with ratings.

domain_description: This database indexes movies and their associated metadata. It stores information about movies (title, budget, revenue, release date, runtime, etc.) and links them to genres, languages (with roles such as original or dubbed), production companies, countries, keywords, and cast/crew members (actors, directors, etc.). Relationships are declared between movies and genres, languages, crew, cast, production companies, and countries. The sample questions show that the database can answer queries about the most common keyword in a given year, movies by language or production company, top-grossing movies, and original language of a specific movie.

term_glossary.language_role: Function of a language for a movie (e.g., original, dubbed)
term_glossary.movie_status: Production status of a movie (e.g., released, post-production)
term_glossary.vote_average: Average user rating score for the movie
term_glossary.cast_order: Ordering rank of an actor's appearance in credits

domain_description: This database stores information about podcasts and their reviews, including podcast metadata (title, iTunes ID, slug, URL) and user reviews (title, content, rating, author, creation date). It also tracks podcast categories and run logs for data ingestion. The database can answer questions about podcast titles by category, review details for specific podcasts, reviewers who wrote particular review titles, review creation dates for a given podcast, and average ratings per podcast category.

term_glossary.itunes_id: Numeric identifier for the podcast on iTunes.
term_glossary.slug: URL-friendly short label for the podcast.
term_glossary.max_rowid: Highest row ID processed in a data run.
term_glossary.reviews_added: Number of reviews added in a data run.

domain_description: This database contains two tables: torrents and tags. The torrents table stores metadata about torrents, including artist, release title (groupName), release year (groupYear), release type, number of times snatched (totalSnatched), and internal identifiers (groupId, id). The tags table holds tag text and its own primary key (index) and an id field. No relationships are declared between the two tables. The database can answer questions about releases by artist, year, release type, and tag-based filtering, though the tag-to-torrent linkage is not explicitly defined.

term_glossary.totalSnatched: Number of times the torrent was downloaded
term_glossary.groupId: Identifier for a group of related torrents

domain_description: This PostgreSQL database stores information about Olympic Games, including the cities and years they were held, participating athletes, sports, events, and medals awarded. Key entities include athletes (person), games editions (games), cities (city), sports (event, sport), medal types (medal), and national Olympic committees (noc_region). Relationships link athletes to games and regions, games to host cities, and events to competitors and medals. The database can answer questions such as which cities hosted multiple games, the youngest participant, games held in a specific city, an athlete's sport, and the most common region of athletes.

term_glossary.noc: National Olympic Committee code, a three-letter abbreviation for a country or region.
term_glossary.noc_region: Table mapping NOC codes to full region names.
term_glossary.games_competitor: Records an athlete's participation in a specific Olympic games at a given age.

domain_description: This database documents professional basketball leagues, likely the NBA, covering players, coaches, teams, awards, drafts, and postseason series. Core entities include players (with biographical and career data), teams (with yearly performance metrics), coaches (with win/loss records), draft picks, all-star game statistics, and various awards for both players and coaches. The schema tracks relationships such as players on teams for given years, coaches assigned to teams, and winners/losers of playoff series. Users can answer historical questions about award counts, draft ages, coaching performance across multiple teams, win/loss comparisons, and team rankings over specified periods.

term_glossary.GP: Games played by a player in a season.
term_glossary.stint: Consecutive tenure segment with same team in a year.
term_glossary.o_fgm: Offensive field goals made by the team.

domain_description: This database stores information about businesses, users, and their interactions from a review platform. The main entities include Business (with location, active status, and star rating), Users (with yelping history, average stars, votes, and review count), Reviews, Tips, and Checkins. Additional supporting entities are Attributes, Categories, Compliments, Days, Years, and several linking tables (Business_Attributes, Business_Categories, Business_Hours, Elite, Users_Compliments). The schema can answer questions about business ratings, categories, locations, check-in patterns, user reviews, compliments, and votes. No relationships are declared explicitly, but foreign keys implied by composite primary keys link businesses to categories, hours, checkins, and reviews; users to reviews, tips, elite status, and compliments.

term_glossary.Elite: Links users to years they were elite members
term_glossary.Checkins: Tracks check-in counts per business and day across 24 hour labels
term_glossary.Business_Attributes: Associates attribute name-value pairs with a business
term_glossary.Business_Categories: Links businesses to their assigned categories
term_glossary.Business_Hours: Stores opening and closing times per business and day
term_glossary.Users_Compliments: Records number of each compliment type a user received
term_glossary.user_average_stars: Average star rating given by the user
term_glossary.user_votes_funny: Count of funny votes received on user's reviews
term_glossary.user_votes_useful: Count of useful votes received on user's reviews
term_glossary.user_votes_cool: Count of cool votes received on user's reviews
term_glossary.user_review_count: Total number of reviews written by the user
term_glossary.user_fans: Number of fans the user has
term_glossary.review_votes_funny: Number of funny votes on a specific review
term_glossary.review_votes_useful: Number of useful votes on a specific review
term_glossary.review_votes_cool: Number of cool votes on a specific review
term_glossary.review_length: Character length of the review text
term_glossary.tip_length: Character length of the tip text
term_glossary.number_of_compliments: Count of a specific compliment type a user has

domain_description: This PostgreSQL database models a retail sales ecosystem, tracking customers, products, store locations, sales teams, geographic regions, and sales orders. It supports queries on order details (e.g., product unit cost or profit), store geography (e.g., cities in a county with latitude/longitude), product performance, customer purchases, and store type comparisons. Key relationships link store locations to regions via StateCode, and sales orders to products and customers through foreign keys (_ProductID, _CustomerID). The schema includes procurement, order, ship, and delivery dates, currency codes, store demographic data (population, income, land/water area), and order quantities with discounts, unit price, and cost. Users can analyze net profit, sales channels, warehouse codes, and more.

term_glossary.ProcuredDate: Date when the product was acquired by the company.
term_glossary.WarehouseCode: Code identifying the warehouse fulfilling the order.
term_glossary.AreaCode: Telephone area code for the store location.
term_glossary._SalesTeamID: Foreign key referencing Sales Team table.
term_glossary._StoreID: Foreign key referencing Store Locations table.

domain_description: This database describes restaurants, their general information, and geographic location. It contains three main entities: 'geographic' (city, county, region), 'generalinfo' (restaurant ID, label, food type, city, numeric review score), and 'location' (restaurant ID, street number/name, city). Declared relationships link generalinfo.city to geographic.city, location.id_restaurant to generalinfo.id_restaurant, and location.city to geographic.city. The database can answer queries about restaurants by city, street, food type, review score, and county, such as finding pizza-serving restaurants in a specific city, listing restaurants on a given street, counting restaurants with a particular review score and street number, or calculating average review scores in a county.

This database records consumer complaints and related data for a retrieval index. It includes client demographics, call center logs, events such as complaints with product and issue details, geographic divisions (states, districts), and product reviews. Key entities are clients, districts, states, call logs, events, and reviews. It can answer questions about client age, regional complaint rates, service outcomes, and complaint details for specific persons or dates. Sample questions involve age differences by region, specific complaint details, client demographics, and dispute percentages by city.

This database supports a retail order management system. It tracks products categorized by type, supplied by vendors, and sold to customers. Employees handle orders, which are shipped via shippers. Key entities include Customers, Employees, Shippers, Suppliers, Categories, Products, Orders, and OrderDetails. Relationships link products to suppliers and categories, orders to customers, employees, and shippers, and order details to products and orders. The schema can answer questions about employee details, customer purchasing patterns, product performance (e.g., highest production in a category), discontinued product percentages, and employee names associated with specific orders.

term_glossary.OrderDetails: Records the quantity of each product in an order.
term_glossary.ShipperID: Identifier for the company that ships the order.

domain_description: This database models a wholesale supplier's order processing and supply chain operations. It includes entities such as customers, orders, line items, parts, suppliers, part-supplier associations, nations, and regions. The schema tracks customer details, orders placed, line items with discount and shipping info, part specifications, supplier accounts, and geographic hierarchies. Queries can address order discounts, clerk assignments, shipping modes, manufacturer part counts, retail price limits, customer-specific order details, and region-based nation listings. Key relationships link orders to customers, line items to orders and to part-supplier pairs, customers to nations, nations to regions, and suppliers to nations.

term_glossary.l_returnflag: Flag indicating if line item was returned (R) or not.
term_glossary.o_shippriority: Shipping priority number assigned to order.
term_glossary.ps_supplycost: Cost to supply the part from supplier.
term_glossary.ps_availqty: Available quantity of part from supplier.

domain_description: This database tracks sales transactions for a retail or manufacturing business. It contains four main entities: Customers, Employees (salespersons), Products, and Sales. The Sales table records each transaction, linking to the product sold, the customer who purchased it, and the employee who made the sale via foreign keys. The schema can answer questions about customer purchase histories, employee sales performance, product popularity, and pricing trends. For example, it can identify which customers bought a specific product (e.g., 'Hex Nut 9'), calculate total sales for a given employee (e.g., Michel E. DeFrance), find customers with unusually large quantities, compute price-based percentages within a range of sales IDs, and determine which employee sold the most of a particular product (e.g., 'Blade'). The database supports aggregations, filtering, and joins across these entities.

This database records sales and weather data, linking stores and weather stations. The main entities are sales_in_weather (daily sales per store and item), weather (daily meteorological readings at each station), and relation (which pairs a store with a weather station). Users can query sales figures on specific dates or under certain weather conditions, compare weather between days, or find extreme weather records at a station. Sample questions refer to store numbers, item numbers, dates, station numbers, and weather attributes such as temperature, wind, and departure from normal.

This database stores information about plays, likely by Shakespeare, structured around works, chapters (acts/scenes), characters, and paragraphs of dialogue. It can answer questions about which character said a specific line, the number of paragraphs per chapter, character appearances in particular paragraphs, scene counts per work and act, and counts of works by genre and date. The main entities are works, chapters, characters, and paragraphs, though no relationships are declared in the schema.

term_glossary.work_id: Foreign key referencing the work to which a chapter belongs.
term_glossary.character_id: Foreign key referencing the character who speaks a paragraph.
term_glossary.chapter_id: Foreign key referencing the chapter containing a paragraph.
term_glossary.Abbrev: Short abbreviation or code for a character's name.
term_glossary.PlainText: Full text content of a paragraph (e.g., dialogue or stage direction).
term_glossary.ParagraphNum: Sequential number of the paragraph within its chapter.
term_glossary.LongTitle: Extended or full title of the work.
term_glossary.GenreType: Literary genre category of the work (e.g., tragedy, comedy).

domain_description: This PostgreSQL database supports a shipping and logistics operation. It records information about cities, customers (businesses that send or receive shipments), drivers, trucks, and shipments. The core fact table 'shipment' links each shipment to a specific customer, truck, driver, and destination city via foreign keys. The schema can answer questions about shipment counts and weights by driver, truck model year, customer, city, or date. For example, it can identify the oldest truck model used, the total weight transported by a particular driver, customer names for shipments in a given month, or the number of trucks from a specific model year. The sample questions refer to driver Sue Newell, truck model years, February 2017 shipments, and shipment weights.

term_glossary.ship_date: Date the shipment was sent or delivered.
term_glossary.area: Geographic area of a city, likely in square miles.
term_glossary.annual_revenue: Customer's yearly revenue in currency units.
term_glossary.zip: Postal code for customer or driver location.

domain_description: This database stores records of incidents, each with a unique case number, date, location, and details about subjects involved (including their statuses, weapons used, and count) and officers. It links to separate tables for officers and subjects, both referencing the incident via case number. It can answer queries about officer demographics (race, gender), subject characteristics and outcomes (e.g., deaths from weapons, statuses), and specific incidents by case number. Users can explore proportions of officer demographics, weapon-related fatalities, and detailed incident information such as victim demographics.

term_glossary.subject_statuses: Status of subjects involved (e.g., Injured, Deceased)
term_glossary.subject_count: Number of subjects involved in the incident

domain_description: This database documents a television series episode retrieval index. It stores episodes with metadata (title, summary, air date, rating, votes), people (cast and crew), awards and nominations (including categories and results), credits (role, category), keywords, and vote distributions. The schema supports queries about episode details, award nominees and their roles, vote counts per episode, and award categories associated with episodes. Relationships link awards to episodes and persons, credits to persons and episodes, keywords to episodes, and votes to episodes. Example questions can ask for birthplaces of producers, vote statistics per episode, episodes by air date and rating, awards nominated by a specific person, or award categories for the most-voted episode.

This database stores cricket match data, including player profiles, teams, venues, and match outcomes. It tracks batting and bowling styles, cities and countries, umpire details, and season awards like Orange and Purple Caps. Entities cover ball-by-ball events, runs scored, wickets taken, extra runs, player roles in matches, and toss decisions. Sample questions can be answered about innings overs, match outcome types, player birth dates and bowling skills, youngest Purple Cap winners, and player roles in specific matches.

term_glossary.Batting_hand: Which hand the batsman uses (e.g., left or right).
term_glossary.Bowling_skill: Type of bowling style (e.g., fast, spin).
term_glossary.Extra_Type_Id: Foreign key referencing the kind of extra run.
term_glossary.Innings_No: Inning number within the match (1 or 2).
term_glossary.Man_of_the_Series: Player awarded best performance over the season.
term_glossary.Orange_Cap: Award for most runs in a season.
term_glossary.Purple_Cap: Award for most wickets in a season.
term_glossary.Rolee: Misspelled 'Role' entity for player match roles.
term_glossary.Toss_Decide: Decision made after winning toss (bat or field).
term_glossary.Win_Type: How the match was won (e.g., by runs or wickets).

domain_description: This PostgreSQL database stores Twitter posts and related metadata for retrieval analysis. The main entities are tweets, users, and locations. Each tweet includes its text, language, engagement metrics (retweets, likes, reach), sentiment score, user Klout score, and timing. Users have a gender attribute. Locations describe cities within states and countries. Declared relationships link tweets to their author and to the location from which they were posted. The database can answer questions about tweet counts by city, top retweeted tweets, user demographics such as gender distribution by state or by retweet activity, and detailed properties of specific tweets.

term_glossary.Klout: Influence score of the Twitter user, higher means more influence.
term_glossary.IsReshare: Boolean indicator whether the tweet is a retweet.
term_glossary.Reach: Number of unique users who potentially saw the tweet.
term_glossary.Sentiment: Numeric score representing positive or negative tone.

domain_description: This database supports a marketing or customer analytics system. It stores demographic data by geographic region (Demog), customer profiles (Customers), and records of mailings (mailings3, Mailings1_2) and sales transactions (Sales). The schema can answer questions about customer demographics, geographic income and population, and relationships between customer attributes and responses or sales. Sample questions involve filtering customers by sex, occupation, age, and geographic income or population.

term_glossary.GEOID: Geographic identifier linking customers to demographic data.
term_glossary.INCOME_K: Average income in thousands of currency units.
term_glossary.REFID: Reference identifier linking mailings and sales.
term_glossary.EVENTID: Unique identifier for a sales event.

domain_description: This database supports a membership organization tracking events, budgets, expenses, income, and members. Core entities include events (with details like type, location, status), members (with contact info and major), budgets (tracking spending by category), expenses (with costs and approval status), income, attendance records, zip codes (including city, county, state), and majors (linked to departments and colleges). Relationships connect members to majors, zip codes, expenses, income, and attendance; events to budgets and attendance; and expenses to budgets. The database can answer questions about approved expenses for specific events, member hometown states, event budget amounts, member spending thresholds, and zip code types by location.

term_glossary.link_to_event: Foreign key referencing the event entity.
term_glossary.link_to_member: Foreign key referencing the member entity.
term_glossary.link_to_budget: Foreign key referencing the budget entity.
term_glossary.link_to_major: Foreign key referencing the major entity.
term_glossary.zip: Postal zip code, linking member to zip_code entity.

domain_description: This database tracks student enrollment, personal attributes, and financial or legal statuses for a school or educational program. Core entities include person (the central individual), bool (a lookup for boolean values), and various status tables like disabled, male, unemployed, filed_for_bankrupcy, and no_payment_due. Relationships link all status tables to person via foreign keys, and no_payment_due references bool for its boolean value. The database can answer questions about counts of students with specific combinations of attributes, such as disabled students with long absences, unemployed and bankrupt students with payment dues, or male disabled students. It also supports queries about enlistment and enrollment details.

term_glossary.bool: A lookup table storing boolean values (e.g., true/false) referenced by other tables.
term_glossary.enlist: Records a person's enlistment, likely in a military or organizational branch.
term_glossary.filed_for_bankrupcy: Indicates a person has filed for bankruptcy.
term_glossary.longest_absense_from_school: Tracks the longest continuous absence from school in months.
term_glossary.no_payment_due: Indicates whether a person has no outstanding payment due, referencing a boolean value.

domain_description: This PostgreSQL database catalogs superheroes and their attributes from various publishers, notably including Marvel Comics. The central entity is 'superhero', which stores names, physical features (eye, hair, skin colour, height, weight), and links to reference tables for gender, race, publisher, and alignment. Supporting tables capture multiple attributes and powers per hero via many-to-many relationships. The schema enables queries about superhero demographics, such as ranking eye colour popularity within a publisher, finding heroes matching specific appearance traits, computing alignment percentages, or listing heroes with missing physical data. It does not include origin stories or team affiliations.

term_glossary.alignment: Moral or ethical category of a superhero (e.g., good, bad, neutral).
term_glossary.hero_attribute: Link table assigning a numeric value for a given attribute to a superhero.
term_glossary.hero_power: Link table associating a superhero with a superpower.
term_glossary.attribute_value: Numerical rating or score for a hero's attribute in the hero_attribute table.

domain_description: This database tracks sales orders from a retail superstore chain operating in four US regions: Central, East, South, and West. It contains customer details, product information, and order transactions stored in separate tables per region. The schema links orders to customers and products via region-based foreign keys. The database can answer questions about customer purchases, product sales, order costs, shipment times, and regional performance.

This database stores electronic health record (EHR) data for a patient cohort. Its main entities are patients, encounters (visits), allergies, care plans, conditions, immunizations, medications, observations, procedures, claims, and a prevalence table for conditions. It tracks patient demographics, clinical events, and administrative data. Relationships link most clinical entities to patients and encounters via foreign keys. The database can answer questions about patient allergies, medication histories, condition prevalence rates, demographic counts, and specific clinical events such as immunizations or procedures. Sample questions involve searching for patients by social security number, full name, medication start year, condition counts, and ethnicity-based allergy counts.

term_glossary.all_prevalences: Table storing disease prevalence statistics across populations.
term_glossary.ENCOUNTER: Unique identifier for a patient visit or interaction.
term_glossary.REASONCODE: Code representing the reason for a clinical action or diagnosis.
term_glossary.REASONDESCRIPTION: Text description corresponding to a reason code.
term_glossary.BILLABLEPERIOD: Date range for which a claim is billed.
term_glossary.ORGANIZATION: Entity responsible for the claim or encounter.

domain_description: This database supports a mobile app analytics platform, tracking user events, app installations, device models, and demographic data. Core entities include events (with timestamps and GPS coordinates), apps, app events (recording installation and activity status), device users (with gender, age, and behavior group), and label categories for app classification. Declared relationships link app events to events and apps, app labels to apps and label categories, and events to device users and their phone brand/model. The database can answer questions about event counts at specific coordinates, app usage per event, counts of apps in a category, average user age per device model, and percentage of users in a behavior group.

term_glossary.app_all: Master table of all application IDs.
term_glossary.is_installed: Integer flag indicating whether the app is installed.
term_glossary.is_active: Integer flag indicating whether the app is currently active.
term_glossary.label_id: Identifier for a category label assigned to an app.
term_glossary.group: Behavior category assigned to a device user.

domain_description: This database stores clinical and laboratory data for patients with autoimmune or thrombotic conditions. It links three main entities: Patient (demographics and admission info), Examination (serological tests like ANA, aCL, LAC, and thrombosis status), and Laboratory (blood chemistry and coagulation markers). Relationships connect both Examination and Laboratory tables to Patient by ID. Queries can explore correlations between lab abnormalities (e.g., uric acid, RBC, IgM, anti-SM), diagnoses, thrombosis severity, and outpatient follow-up.

term_glossary.KCT: Kaolin clotting time, a lupus anticoagulant test
term_glossary.RVVT: Russell viper venom time, a lupus anticoagulant test

domain_description: This database models molecules and their structural components. The main entities are molecules (with a label), atoms (with an element type), and bonds (with a bond type). The schema records which atoms belong to which molecule, which bonds link atoms, and the connectivity between atoms via bonds. It can answer queries about atom composition, bond types (e.g., double bonds), connectivity within molecules, and molecule-level properties such as carcinogenicity. The sample questions refer to molecule numbering, bond identification, and atom relationships within specific molecules.

term_glossary.bond_type: Type or order of a chemical bond (e.g., single, double)

domain_description: This database models a train collection for a retrieval index, capturing trains and their constituent cars. The 'trains' entity stores each train's identifier and running direction. The 'cars' entity represents individual railcars linked to a train via a foreign key, recording their shape, side/roof descriptions, length, wheel count, and optional load shape and load number. The schema can answer questions about train directions, car counts per train, wheel totals for cars of specific lengths, and distributions such as the proportion of trains meeting a minimum car threshold. Queries may involve aggregations like averages, percentages, and majority direction analysis.

term_glossary.id: Unique identifier for each train or car record.
term_glossary.len: Length of a car, likely as text (e.g., 'short' or 'long').
term_glossary.load_shape: Shape of the cargo carried by a car (e.g., 'box', 'cylinder').
term_glossary.load_num: Number of load units on a car, if applicable.
term_glossary.train_id: Foreign key linking a car to its parent train.

domain_description: This database tracks universities, their rankings, and student demographics across years. It contains entities for countries, universities, ranking systems (e.g., Publications Rank), ranking criteria, and yearly ranking scores. It also stores yearly university statistics such as total students, student-staff ratio, and percentages of international and female students. Relationships link universities to their country, ranking criteria to their system, and yearly records to their university and ranking criteria. The database can answer questions about top-performing universities, trends in international student enrollment, specific ranking criteria IDs, student size thresholds, and filtering by country or score.

term_glossary.pct_international_students: Percentage of students who are international at a university in a given year.
term_glossary.pct_female_students: Percentage of students who are female at a university in a given year.
term_glossary.student_staff_ratio: Ratio of students to academic staff at a university in a given year.

domain_description: This PostgreSQL database records video game releases and sales across platforms and regions. The main entities are games, genres, platforms, publishers, and sales regions. Games belong to one genre and can be published by multiple publishers through a game_publisher junction. Each game-publisher combination can be released on multiple platforms in different years via game_platform. Sales figures are stored per region and per game-platform combination. The schema supports questions about popular platforms in specific regions, game counts per platform, game genres, and earliest release years.

term_glossary.game_publisher: Links a game to one of its publishers.
term_glossary.game_platform: Links a game-publisher pair to a platform and release year.
term_glossary.region_sales: Records sales numbers for a game-platform in a region.

domain_description: This database supports a retail or manufacturing business, tracking products, sales, purchasing, employees, and customers. Core entities include Product, SalesOrderHeader, Customer, Employee, Vendor, and BusinessEntity. It can answer questions about product inventory, sales orders, employee history, vendor performance, and manufacturing details. For example, it can find total ordered quantities for a product line, shelf locations for specific products, count of in-house manufactured salable items, departmental history of an employee, and profit analysis by vendor.

term_glossary.rowguid: Globally unique identifier for the row, used for replication.
term_glossary.BusinessEntityID: Unique identifier for a person, store, or vendor.
term_glossary.SalariedFlag: Indicates whether an employee is salaried (1) or hourly (0).
term_glossary.CurrentFlag: Indicates whether the employee is currently active (1) or not (0).
term_glossary.MakeFlag: Indicates whether the product is manufactured in-house (1) or purchased (0).
term_glossary.FinishedGoodsFlag: Indicates whether the product is a finished good (1) or component (0).
term_glossary.SafetyStockLevel: Minimum inventory quantity to maintain for a product.
term_glossary.ReorderPoint: Inventory level that triggers a reorder for the product.
term_glossary.BOMLevel: Level of the component in the bill of materials hierarchy.
term_glossary.PerAssemblyQty: Quantity of the component used per assembly.
term_glossary.ScrappedQty: Quantity of product scrapped during manufacturing.
term_glossary.StockedQty: Quantity of product stocked after receiving or manufacturing.

domain_description: This database stores geographic and demographic information about countries, cities, and languages. It includes details such as population, capital cities, government forms, and official languages. The main entities are City, Country, and CountryLanguage. Relationships link cities and languages to their respective countries via foreign keys. Users can ask questions about which country has the most populous city, capital cities of large countries, countries using a specific language, leadership of countries containing a district, or counts of governments with a specific form.

term_glossary.GNP: Gross National Product, an economic measure for a country.
term_glossary.GNPOld: Previous year's Gross National Product for comparison.
term_glossary.LifeExpectancy: Average expected lifespan of a country's population.
term_glossary.SurfaceArea: Total land area of a country, usually in square kilometers.
term_glossary.Code2: Two-letter country code, often ISO alpha-2.
term_glossary.HeadOfState: Title or name of the country's head of state.

domain_description: This database stores macroeconomic and social indicators for countries, sourced from the World Bank. The main entities are Country (with attributes like region, income group, currency, and census years), Series (indicator metadata such as topic, definition, periodicity, and license type), and fact tables (Indicators, CountryNotes, Footnotes, SeriesNotes) that link countries to series with values and annotations. Foreign keys connect country codes and series codes across tables. The schema can answer questions about country classifications (e.g., income group, currency), indicator values over time, census and registration completion, and license types. Queries can compare indicator values across countries or years, or count shares of countries meeting certain criteria.

term_glossary.SnaPriceValuation: System of National Accounts price valuation method.
term_glossary.PppSurveyYear: Year of the purchasing power parity survey.
term_glossary.BalanceOfPaymentsManualInUse: Manual edition used for balance of payments.
term_glossary.ExternalDebtReportingStatus: Status of external debt data reporting.
term_glossary.ImfDataDisseminationStandard: IMF data dissemination standard (e.g., SDDS, GDDS).
term_glossary.LatestPopulationCensus: Year of the most recent population census.
term_glossary.LatestHouseholdSurvey: Year of the most recent household survey.
term_glossary.SourceOfMostRecentIncomeAndExpenditureDa: Data source for latest income/expenditure survey.
term_glossary.VitalRegistrationComplete: Indicates if vital registration is complete (yes/no).
term_glossary.LatestAgriculturalCensus: Year of the most recent agricultural census.
term_glossary.LatestIndustrialData: Year of the most recent industrial data.
term_glossary.LatestTradeData: Year of the most recent trade data.
term_glossary.LatestWaterWithdrawalData: Year of the most recent water withdrawal data.
term_glossary.TableName: Short table name for the country.
term_glossary.LongName: Full official name of the country.
